negative_search_evidence.missing_behavior: No canonical scheduling path computes a single next/current execution index, compares it to fired_count, and skips missed catch-up indices; the port can synthesize multiple unexecuted due indices in one block/window.
related_but_insufficient_code: The port due_index_bounds/compute_obligations path can emit every unexecuted index in a due TTL window, with tests asserting multi-index due windows. Payload building consumes that list and has no current-fired-count filter.

notes: Confirmed gap; adjacent guards exist, but no-catch-up/current-fired-count invariant is not preserved.
rationale: The canonical scheduling candidates collapse into a current dispatch invariant: local canonical processing fires one transaction for the next unfired index only, skips missed windows rather than catching up, and guards zero frequency.
statement: Canonical scheduling must emit a scheduled transaction only when the block's computed execution index equals the call's current fired count, must skip already-fired, future, exhausted, missed catch-up, and pre-start windows, and must skip frequency == 0 calls before any division.
suggested_falsification_tests: Process a call with start_block 100, frequency 1, and num_calls 1 across a multi-block TTL window and assert only one scheduled transaction is submitted.
Advance a frequency-5 call past missed windows and assert canonical processing does not emit catch-up transactions for skipped indices.
Inject a frequency == 0 call and assert canonical processing does not panic or submit a transaction.
summary: Canonical scheduling fires-only-next-index behavior is absent.
test_or_falsification_candidate: Scheduler state with start_block=100, frequency=1, ttl=500, no executed bits at block 105 should produce 0..=5 in the port but invariant expects at most one next index.
title: Canonical scheduling fires only the next index
